title: Syzygium casiguranense
intro: Syzygium casiguranense, also known as the Casiguran tree or Philippine water apple, is a species of flowering plant in the family Myrtaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, cough, and diarrhea, due to its potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Syzygium casiguranense include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which have been identified as responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Syzygium casiguranense is commonly prepared as a tea or infusions, as well as being used in the form of decoctions and infusions to treat various health conditions, although further research is needed to confirm its efficacy and safety.
taxonomy: Syzygium casiguranense is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Myrtaceae. It is native to the Philippines, specifically in the Cagayan Valley region. The plant is a member of the genus Syzygium, which is a diverse group of plants commonly known as "water apple" or "jambu" trees. The Myrtaceae family is a large and widespread group of plants that includes many species of trees and shrubs. Syzygium casiguranense is considered an endangered species due to habitat loss and degradation.
